metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = photon energy above threshold, electron ejected successfully; CRIMSON = photon energy below threshold, no ejection regardless of intensity
metadata.note: Exclusions: no work-function measurement lab, no stopping-potential circuit, no Millikan's full experimental setup, no Einstein 1905 paper history beyond one sentence

beats.narration_text: A metal surface under red light. Turn up the power as high as you like. Not one electron leaves.

card.headline:
Why Bright Red Light
Ejects No Electrons

beats.narration_text: Robert Millikan in 1914: double the intensity of red light on sodium. The ammeter reads zero. Switch to dim ultraviolet — electrons fly out immediately.
Classical theory says a brighter wave delivers more energy. Electrons should accumulate that energy and eventually escape. They never do.

card.headline: A brighter wave delivers more energy per second. Classical theory predicts electrons should accumulate and escape. Here zero electrons emerge under intense red but electrons fly out under dim violet. Why?

beats.narration_text: Classical physics treats light as a continuous wave. More intensity means more energy delivered per second, per square centimeter. An electron should soak it up and go.

But experiments show the wrong variable matters. Kinetic energy of ejected electrons tracks frequency, not intensity. Below a sharp threshold frequency — nothing. Above it — electrons, each with the same energy.
So why is frequency the key, not power?

Einstein proposed light itself comes in packets — photons. Each photon carries energy h-nu, where nu is its frequency. One photon hits one electron. Either the packet has enough energy to clear the binding threshold, or it does not.
Intensity just controls how many photons arrive per second. Below the threshold frequency, a million photons hit and none succeed. Above it, one dim photon succeeds every time.
The threshold is sharp because energy arrives in units, not as a trickle.

card.headline:
One photon, one electron —
or nothing

beats.narration_text: Every metal has a work function — the binding energy an electron must overcome. If the photon energy h-nu exceeds that barrier, the electron escapes with whatever is left over.
This is why frequency controls the outcome. Higher frequency means higher energy per photon. The threshold is set by the barrier, not by the power of the source.

props.headline: This is why frequency controls the outcome. Higher frequency means higher…

beats.narration_text: The implication is stark: every quantum of light either clears the bar or it does not. There is no partial credit, no waiting, no accumulation across multiple photons.

card.headline:
Sodium under red
and violet light

beats.narration_text: Sodium work function 2.36 eV. Red at 620 nm: photon energy 2.0 eV — below the bar. Zero electrons, even at 10 milliwatts. Switch to violet at 400 nm: photon energy 3.1 eV — above the bar by 0.74 eV. Every photon launches one electron at 0.74 eV. Same result at 0.1 milliwatts. Intensity changed a hundredfold. Energy per electron did not.
Below threshold: no electrons at any power. Above threshold: every photon ejects one electron with fixed energy. Frequency decides. Intensity never does. That is the photoelectric effect.

card.headline: Below threshold: no electrons, any intensity. Above: every photon ejects one electron at fixed energy. Frequency decides. Intensity never does.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
